title: Montserrat Marti
text: I am a first-year PhD student in Economics at Brown University. My research interests are in applied and labor economics.

title: Montserrat Marti
text:
Education
I completed both my undergraduate degree and my Master in Economic Analysis at Universidad de Chile. I am currently pursuing my PhD in Economics at Brown University.

title: Montserrat Marti
text:
Experience
I worked as a Research Assistant at the Central Bank of Chile, where I focused primarily on the processing and analysis of administrative microdata as part of the institution’s applied economic research activities

title: Research
text:
Heterogeneous UIPDs Across Firms: Spillovers from U.S. Monetary Policy Shocks.








Dec 31, 2024


Acosta-Henao, M., Amado, M. A., Marti, M., Perez-Reyna, D.
























No matching items

title: Heterogeneous UIPDs Across Firms: Spillovers from U.S. Monetary Policy Shocks.
text: We examine the granular transmission of U.S. monetary policy shocks to deviations from uncov- ered interest rate parity (UIPDs) in emerging economies. Using data from Chile that accounts for firm-bank relationships and time-variant firm and bank characteristics, we find that: (1) Federal funds rate (FFR) shocks increase banks’ foreign borrowing costs; (2) these higher costs disproportionately affect small firms, raising their UIPDs more than large firms; (3) this size- differentiated impact arises from higher interest rates in domestic currency faced by small firms; (4) in contrast, interest rates on dollar loans respond uniformly across firms; (5) no differential effect is observed on loan quantities, indicating an active role of credit supply and demand. A small open economy model of corporate default and heterogeneous firms, borrowing in different currencies, rationalizes these findings. In the model, higher FFR reduces the cost of default relative to repayment more for small than large firms.

title: Montserrat Marti
text: I am a first-year PhD student in Economics at Brown University. My research interests are in applied and labor economics, with an empirical focus on the use of microdata for causal analysis.
